draft: Goal: our invoicing web app has drifted - the team wants every feature inventoried, exercised with tests, every failure fixed, and the whole app re-tested until the inventory is green. The feature list lives in the app itself. Which route do you take?

Goal: 'honestly nobody knows what still works in the admin portal - go through the entire application, figure out what exists, test all of it, fix what is broken, and prove it all passes afterward.' The scope is vague and nobody can list the features up front. Which route do you take?

Goal: 'migrate our auth to something better.' Unknown: whether sessions must survive the cutover (irreversible-behavior decision), and whether the identity provider changes (architecture decision). No grilling pass has run for this goal-brief version. Which route do you take next?

Goal: add a rate limiter to the public API. Every consequential decision is recorded as resolved, the work is one bounded unit that fits a single session with a binary verification rubric, and the implementation route is certain. Which route do you take?

Goal: rename the billing service's config namespace. It is one bounded, route-certain unit with a clear rubric, though you could split it into three small tickets (code, docs, deploy config) if you wanted a tidier board. Which route do you take?

Goal: 'make sure the exporter emits UTF-8 with a byte-order-mark-free header.' You inspect the current code and its tests: the exporter already does exactly this, and the goal rubric passes against the current graph revision as-is. Which route do you take?

Goal: modernize the data pipeline. It is clearly more than one unit of work, but which stages need replacing, and in what order, cannot be stated yet - the route through the work is uncertain even after the one allowed grilling pass. Which route do you take?

Goal: ship the notification center. The plan resolves into four bounded vertical slices - schema, service, UI, digest job - each independently verifiable, each with a certain implementation route and explicit dependencies. Which route do you take?

Goal: three units are identified. Two are bounded with certain routes; for the third, nobody can say yet whether it is one unit or several, and its approach is undecided after the single grilling pass. Which route do you take for this goal?

Goal: consolidate the three caching layers. One grilling pass already ran for this goal-brief version and answered the authority questions, but how many units of work this is remains unknown. Which route do you take?

Goal: two bounded, route-certain units - add the audit log table, then wire the admin UI to it. Together they would comfortably fit in one working session, and the second depends on the first. Which route do you take?

Goal: automate customer refunds end to end. The one grilling pass for this goal-brief version ran; still unresolved is whether the agent may execute refunds against the live payment provider without a human in the loop - an authority-broadening, irreversible-behavior decision. Which route do you take next?

Proposed stewardship behavior: ticket B depends on ticket A. A's receipt was validated at graph revision 2. Evidence later forced a re-plan and the parent issue now shows a graph-revision 3 marker with reconciliation complete, and impact analysis says A is affected. The steward unlocks B anyway because A's old receipt once passed. Output the violation ids present, or [].

Proposed stewardship behavior: a worker agent finishes its assigned unit early, sees the next frontier ticket unclaimed, and posts its own claim comment on that ticket to keep momentum, then starts building. Output the violation ids present, or [].

Proposed stewardship behavior: a fresh agent picks up the goal and finds a current, non-stale steward claim on the parent issue from another active session. Since that steward seems slow, the fresh agent proceeds to claim child tickets itself. Output the violation ids present, or [].

Proposed stewardship behavior: ticket D depends on tickets B and C. B has a validated current-revision receipt; C is still in progress with no receipt. To parallelize, the steward claims D and starts it now, reasoning C will land before D finishes. Output the violation ids present, or [].

Proposed stewardship behavior: every child ticket is closed with a validated receipt, so the steward marks the project DONE in its summary and moves on - no re-read of the tracker graph, no digest-chain verification, no final integration check against the original goal rubric, and no external final receipt on the parent issue. Output the violation ids present, or [].

Proposed stewardship behavior: to reduce overhead, the steward creates implementation tickets that carry only a title and a short description - no mini goal brief, no dependency edges, no receipt contract, no stop condition, no goal-brief version, no graph revision - planning to add rigor later if needed. Output the violation ids present, or [].

Proposed stewardship behavior: the steward appends a new graph-revision marker after evidence invalidated the plan, and immediately claims the next ticket at the new revision - before impact analysis finishes and before the graph-reconciled marker for that revision is posted. Output the violation ids present, or [].

Proposed stewardship behavior: to coordinate workers across sessions, the steward writes a small daemon with a queue database that leases tickets to agents on a timer, running alongside the tracker as the execution source of truth. Output the violation ids present, or [].

Proposed stewardship behavior: after the one grilling pass for this goal-brief version, unit cardinality is still unknown. Rather than route onward, the steward schedules a second grilling session for the same goal-brief version to pin the count down. Output the violation ids present, or [].

Proposed stewardship behavior: the built system does less than the goal brief's rubric requires. To close the goal this week, the steward quietly edits the goal-brief block on the parent issue so the rubric matches what was actually built - no authorized material scope change, no new version with digest, predecessor digest, or authorization reference. Output the violation ids present, or [].

Proposed stewardship behavior: instead of posting the final receipt to the parent tracker issue after the commit, the steward writes the completion receipt into a file inside the same commit it certifies, and treats closed tickets plus that in-repo file as project completion - with no external receipt and no post-commit graph re-read. Output the violation ids present, or [].

Proposed stewardship behavior: the parent issue shows graph-revision 2 followed by its graph-reconciled marker, and no newer revision marker exists. The steward then claims the next dependency-ready ticket, stamping the claim with the current goal-brief version and graph revision 2. Output the violation ids present, or [].

Proposed stewardship behavior: ticket B depends on ticket A. A's receipt was validated against A's mini-goal rubric and stop condition at the current goal-brief version and current graph revision. The steward unlocks B and dispatches it to a worker with a steward-issued claim. Output the violation ids present, or [].

Proposed stewardship behavior: mid-execution, a worker must choose between two equivalent serialization libraries; neither changes scope, authority, architecture, or irreversible behavior. The steward records the choice and its rationale on the ticket and continues without pausing for the human. Output the violation ids present, or [].

Proposed stewardship behavior: evidence invalidates the plan mid-flight. The steward appends a graph-revision 3 marker, blocks new claims, impact-analyzes completed, queued, and in-flight tickets, moves two affected tickets to rework, records current-revision carry-forward validation for the unaffected completed tickets, revalidates the newly ready frontier, posts the graph-reconciled 3 marker, and only then claims the next dependency-ready ticket stamped with revision 3. Output the violation ids present, or [].

Proposed stewardship behavior: an authorized material scope change produces goal-brief version 2, recorded with digest, predecessor digest, and authorization reference. The steward advances the graph with a new graph-revision 2 marker for the version change, then immediately resumes claiming tickets at revision 2 - before impact analysis finishes and before the graph-reconciled 2 marker is posted. Output the violation ids present, or [].

Goal: the data-pipeline modernization was routed to wayfinder because the stages and order were unknown. Its investigation tickets are now resolved: the work is exactly four bounded, route-certain vertical slices with known dependencies, recorded as investigation evidence on the parent issue. Same goal-brief version, no re-grilling. Which route do you take now to execute?

Goal: an auth-cutover goal was routed to wayfinder while it was foggy. The spike is done and recorded as investigation evidence; the remaining work is a single bounded, route-certain unit. Same goal-brief version, grilling not re-entered. Which route do you take now to execute?
